description_long: This object is a detailed stone sculpture of a mythological Chinese guardian creature, often referred to as a Qilin or Foo Dog, standing on a rectangular plinth. The creature features a highly ornate mane, expressive facial features with an open mouth, and a scaled body with muscular limbs. Its tail is bushy and curled, and the legs are positioned as if in mid-stride, giving a sense of motion. The base is decorated with stylized cloud motifs and includes a gold-colored inscription on one side. The entire sculpture is rendered in a grayish stone material, with intricate carvings and a robust, solid appearance.
description: A highly detailed stone guardian creature sculpture with ornate features, standing on a decorated rectangular base.
description_view_0: Front view showing the creature's expressive face, open mouth, and detailed mane, with both front legs visible.
description_view_1: Side view highlighting the scaled body, bushy tail, and gold inscription on the base, with the creature in a dynamic pose.
description_view_2: Rear view displaying the back legs, tail, and the textured surface of the mane and body, with less detail on the face.
description_view_3: Opposite side view showing the other profile of the creature, the flowing mane, and the decorative base.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the entrance of urban buildings, parks, or historical sites, often flanking gateways or driveways as a symbolic protective barrier.
attribute_installation_orientation: Fully above ground, upright on a flat surface such as a plinth or directly on pavement.
attribute_wear_patterns: Likely to show smoothing and slight discoloration on protruding surfaces such as the head, mane, and limbs due to weathering and occasional contact. Edges of the base may exhibit minor chipping from incidental impacts or foot traffic.
attribute_historical_markings: Gold-colored inscription on the side of the base, likely in Chinese characters, with no other visible engravings or symbols.
